summary: DeVault introduces pass-rotate, a tool he built to automate bulk password rotation across multiple websites. Motivated by the CloudFlare memory leak incident that potentially compromised accounts on thousands of sites, he created a youtube-dl-style utility that reverse engineers password change flows to rotate credentials in seconds rather than hours. The tool integrates with password managers and supports two-factor authentication methods. He encourages contributions to expand the list of supported services and proposes standardizing automated password rotation.
key_points: Mass security events like CloudFlare's leak create an impractical burden on users who need to rotate dozens of passwords manually
pass-rotate works by reverse engineering each website's password change flow via HTTP requests, similar to how youtube-dl handles video extraction
The tool is designed as both a standalone CLI and a library that password managers can integrate directly
Two-factor authentication (TOTP, SMS, U2F) is handled interactively during rotation
money_quotes: I just changed 4 passwords in about 20 seconds.
This is pass-rotate, which is basically youtube-dl for rotating passwords.
Updating a single password can take 5 minutes, and changing dozens of them might take hours.
Not only can it help you rotate passwords after security events, but it can be used for periodic password rotation to keep your accounts safer in general.
This kind of project needs long term maintenance to support countless providers and keep up with changes to them.
key_insight: Mass security breaches expose a fundamental UX failure in password management that can be solved by automating the password rotation workflow across providers.
